text:
CT CHEST/ABDOMEN/PELVIS WITH CONTRAST AND WHOLE-BODY BONE SCINTIGRAPHY

CLINICAL INDICATION:
38-year-old woman with newly diagnosed left breast mass, biopsy-confirmed invasive ductal carcinoma (triple-negative, grade 3, Ki-67 85%), and biopsy-confirmed left axillary lymph node metastasis. Studies ordered for systemic staging to evaluate for distant metastatic disease prior to initiation of neoadjuvant therapy.

---

PART I: CT CHEST/ABDOMEN/PELVIS WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST

TECHNIQUE:
Helical CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis was performed with intravenous iodinated contrast (Omnipaque 350, 100 mL) administered via antecubital vein, following a standard triple-phase protocol. Axial images were reconstructed at 3 mm slice thickness with coronal and sagittal reformats. Oral contrast was not administered. Comparison: No prior cross-sectional imaging available.

FINDINGS:

CHEST:
Lungs and Airways: The lungs are clear and well-aerated bilaterally. No pulmonary nodules, masses, or consolidation. No ground-glass opacities. The airways are patent to the subsegmental level. No endobronchial lesions.

Pleura/Pericardium: No pleural effusions. No pneumothorax. No pericardial effusion.

Mediastinum and Hila: The mediastinum is midline. No anterior, middle, or posterior mediastinal masses. No paratracheal, subcarinal, or hilar lymphadenopathy. Prevascular nodes are normal in size (largest 5 mm short-axis). The thymus appears involuted, normal for age.

Heart and Great Vessels: Heart size is normal. No intracardiac masses. The thoracic aorta is normal in caliber. Pulmonary arteries are normal.

Chest Wall and Soft Tissues: Left breast: An irregular, spiculated mass is noted in the left upper outer quadrant corresponding to the known primary carcinoma, measuring approximately 3 cm in greatest dimension on this modality (dedicated breast imaging is the reference standard). Left axillary region: An enlarged left axillary lymph node is identified measuring approximately 2.0 cm in short axis, with loss of the normal fatty hilum, consistent with the known biopsy-confirmed nodal metastasis. No additional suspicious left axillary, left internal mammary, or left supraclavicular lymphadenopathy beyond this single confirmed node. Right breast and right axilla are unremarkable with no suspicious masses or lymphadenopathy.

Bones (chest): Thoracic spine and ribs are visualized without focal lytic or sclerotic lesions. No pathologic-appearing osseous abnormalities.

ABDOMEN:
Liver: The liver is normal in size and morphology with a smooth surface contour. No focal hepatic lesions identified. No biliary ductal dilation. The portal and hepatic veins are patent.

Gallbladder and Biliary System: Gallbladder is normal without cholelithiasis, wall thickening, or pericholecystic fluid. Bile ducts are not dilated.

Spleen: Normal size and attenuation. No splenic lesions.

Pancreas: Normal in morphology and attenuation throughout head, body, and tail. Pancreatic duct is not dilated.

Kidneys and Ureters: Kidneys are symmetric in size and position with normal parenchymal thickness and corticomedullary differentiation. No hydronephrosis. No renal masses or calculi. Normal bilateral adrenal glands without nodularity or enlargement.

Abdominal Vasculature: The abdominal aorta is normal in caliber. No para-aortic, aortocaval, or retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy. No mesenteric lymphadenopathy.

Peritoneum and Bowel: No ascites. No peritoneal deposits or omental thickening. Bowel loops are unremarkable without wall thickening or obstruction.

PELVIS:
Uterus and Ovaries: The uterus is normal in size and shape. No adnexal masses. The ovaries are not clearly defined on this non-dedicated study; dedicated pelvic ultrasound may be considered if clinically indicated.

Lymph Nodes: No pelvic or inguinal lymphadenopathy. No iliac chain adenopathy.

Bladder: Urinary bladder is decompressed; wall appears normal.

Bones (abdomen/pelvis): Lumbar spine, sacrum, iliac wings, and proximal femora visualized without convincing focal lytic or sclerotic lesions. No pathologic fractures.

---

PART II: WHOLE-BODY BONE SCINTIGRAPHY (Tc-99m MDP)

TECHNIQUE:
Whole-body planar bone scintigraphy was performed 3 hours following intravenous injection of Tc-99m methylene diphosphonate (MDP), approximately 25 mCi. Anterior and posterior whole-body projections were obtained. Spot views of the thorax and pelvis were acquired for additional characterization. SPECT images were not obtained.

FINDINGS:
Physiologic tracer distribution is present in the axial and appendicular skeleton, kidneys, and bladder. Soft tissue tracer activity is within normal limits.

No focal areas of abnormal radiotracer uptake are identified in the cervical, thoracic, or lumbar spine. The ribs, sternum, and clavicles are without focal abnormal uptake. The pelvis including the iliac wings, sacrum, and proximal femora demonstrate symmetric, physiologic uptake without focal abnormalities. The calvarium, long bones of the upper and lower extremities, and hands and feet are unremarkable.

No photopenic defects suggestive of purely lytic lesions are appreciated.

Note: Mild symmetric increased uptake is seen at the bilateral knees and left shoulder, consistent with degenerative changes. No aggressive or malignant-appearing osseous uptake pattern is identified.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. No pulmonary nodules, parenchymal masses, or pleural disease to suggest intrathoracic metastatic involvement.

2. Known left breast primary mass and single enlarged left axillary lymph node as previously characterized on dedicated breast imaging; no additional suspicious thoracic or abdominal nodal disease identified.

3. No hepatic lesions. No adrenal, retroperitoneal, or pelvic masses suspicious for metastatic disease.

4. Whole-body bone scintigraphy is negative for focal abnormal osseous uptake. The skeletal survey on CT likewise shows no convincing lytic, sclerotic, or destructive osseous lesions.

5. Taken together, these studies demonstrate no imaging evidence of distant organ or skeletal metastatic disease. Disease appears confined to the left breast and ipsilateral axillary nodal basin based on current evaluation.
